revision.text:
'''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。
== 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、八咫烏の先導により、[[大久米命]]（大来米命、おおくめのみこと）と共に兵を率いて[[宇陀]]（菟田）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。
兄猾（兄宇迦斯、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。

'''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。
== 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、[[八咫烏]]の先導により、[[大久米命]]（[[大来米命]]、おおくめのみこと）と共に兵を率いて[[菟田]]（[[宇陀]]）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。
[[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。
国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。

'''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。
== 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、[[八咫烏]]の先導により、[[大久米命]]（[[大来米命]]、おおくめのみこと）と共に兵を率いて[[菟田]]（[[宇陀]]）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。
[[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。
国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。
[[Category:日本神話の人物|みちのおみのみこと]]

'''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。
== 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、[[八咫烏]]の先導により[[久米氏|久米部]]を率いて[[菟田]]（[[宇陀]]）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。
[[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。
国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。
その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。
神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。
[[Category:日本神話の人物|みちのおみのみこと]]

神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。

[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|道臣命 ／『前賢故実』より]] '''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。「古屋家家譜」<ref>『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』浅間神社 1979年,pp278-300。）資料篇所収。宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』（古代氏族研究会、1986年）も『古屋家家譜』の内容を採用している。</ref>では[[刺田比古]]の子。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。

神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。

[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|道臣命 ／『前賢故実』より]] '''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。「古屋家家譜」<ref>『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』浅間神社 1979年,pp278-300。）資料篇所収。宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』（古代氏族研究会、1986年）も『古屋家家譜』の内容を採用している。</ref>では[[刺田比古]]の子。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。
== 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、[[八咫烏]]の先導により[[久米氏|久米部]]を率いて[[菟田]]（[[宇陀]]）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。
[[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。
国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。
その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。
神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。
== 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇]] *[[大伴氏]] *[[神武景気]] *[[神武天皇即位紀元]] *[[刺田比古神社]] *[[伴林氏神社]] ==外部リンク== *[http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02103.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大伴氏／伴氏【1】] *[http://www.jtw.zaq.ne.jp/okanomiya/ 刺田比古神社]
[[Category:日本神話の人物|みちのおみのみこと]]

[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|道臣命　／『前賢故実』より]] '''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。「古屋家家譜」『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』浅間神社　1979年,pp278-300。）資料篇所収。宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』（古代氏族研究会、1986年）も『古屋家家譜』の内容を採用している。では[[刺田比古]]の子。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。 == 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、[[八咫烏]]の先導により[[久米氏|久米部]]を率いて[[菟田]]（[[宇陀]]）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。 [[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。 国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。 その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。 神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。 なお、『[[上記]]（ウエツフミ）』では、上記の逸話はすべて、神武天皇の伯父の大久米の命に関する記述である。つまり道臣命とは、大久米の命のことと考えられる。記紀は神武天皇の先代・第71代ウガヤフキアエズ天皇の存在を認めていないので、その弟君である大久米の命も宙に浮いてしまうことになり、道臣命という架空の人物を創作せざるを得なくなったと考えられる。 == 道臣命を祭神とする神社 == *[[刺田比古神社]]（[[和歌山市]]） *[[伴林氏神社]]（[[藤井寺市]]） == 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇]] *[[大伴氏]] *[[神武景気]] *[[神武天皇即位紀元]] ==外部リンク== *[http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02103.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観　大伴氏／伴氏【1】] *[http://www.jtw.zaq.ne.jp/okanomiya/ 刺田比古神社] [[Category:日本神話の人物|みちのおみのみこと]]

[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|道臣命　／『前賢故実』より]] '''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。「古屋家家譜」『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』浅間神社　1979年,pp278-300。）資料篇所収。宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』（古代氏族研究会、1986年）も『古屋家家譜』の内容を採用している。では[[刺田比古]]の子。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。 == 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、[[八咫烏]]の先導により[[久米氏|久米部]]を率いて[[菟田]]（[[宇陀]]）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。 [[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。 国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。 その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。 神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。 なお、『[[上記]]（ウエツフミ）』では、上記の逸話はすべて、神武天皇の伯父の大久米の命に関する記述である。つまり道臣命とは、大久米の命(別名:ウマシウチの命)のことと考えられる。記紀は神武天皇の先代・第71代ウガヤフキアエズ天皇の存在を認めていないので、その弟君である大久米の命も宙に浮いてしまうことになり、道臣命という架空の人物を創作せざるを得なくなったと考えられる。なお第71代と大久米の命のあいだには高倉下の命がいる。 == 道臣命を祭神とする神社 == *[[刺田比古神社]]（[[和歌山市]]） *[[伴林氏神社]]（[[藤井寺市]]） == 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇]] *[[大伴氏]] *[[神武景気]] *[[神武天皇即位紀元]] ==外部リンク== *[http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02103.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観　大伴氏／伴氏【1】] *[http://www.jtw.zaq.ne.jp/okanomiya/ 刺田比古神社] [[Category:日本神話の人物|みちのおみのみこと]]

revision.comment: 平成27年2月12日の版に巻き戻し：偽書の俗伝であり、特筆性に欠けると判断。併せてWikipediaのルールに沿わない外部リンク除去。
revision.text:
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|道臣命 ／『前賢故実』より]] '''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。「古屋家家譜」<ref>『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』浅間神社 1979年,pp278-300。）資料篇所収。宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』（古代氏族研究会、1986年）も『古屋家家譜』の内容を採用している。</ref>では[[刺田比古]]の子。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。
== 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、[[八咫烏]]の先導により[[久米氏|久米部]]を率いて[[菟田]]（[[宇陀]]）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。
[[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。
国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。
その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。
神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。
== 道臣命を祭神とする神社 == *[[刺田比古神社]]（[[和歌山市]]） *[[伴林氏神社]]（[[藤井寺市]]）
== 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇]] *[[大伴氏]] *[[神武景気]] *[[神武天皇即位紀元]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.jtw.zaq.ne.jp/okanomiya/ 刺田比古神社]
[[Category:日本神話の人物|みちのおみのみこと]]

その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。
神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。
== 道臣命を祭神とする神社 == *[[刺田比古神社]]（[[和歌山市]]） *[[伴林氏神社]]（[[藤井寺市]]） *[[住吉大伴神社]] ([[京都市]])
== 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇]] *[[大伴氏]] *[[神武景気]] *[[神武天皇即位紀元]]

[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|道臣命 ／『前賢故実』より]] '''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は記紀に登場する人物。初名は日臣命（ひのおみのみこと）。[[天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔。「古屋家家譜」<ref>『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』浅間神社 1979年,pp278-300。）資料篇所収。宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』（古代氏族研究会、1986年）も『古屋家家譜』の内容を採用している。</ref>では[[刺田比古]]の子。[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。

[[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。
国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。
その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。
神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。
== 道臣命を祭神とする神社 == *[[刺田比古神社]]（[[和歌山市]]） *[[伴林氏神社]]（[[藤井寺市]]） *[[住吉大伴神社]] ([[京都市]]) *蹴裂権現社([[大分県]])
== 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇]] *[[大伴氏]] *[[神武景気]] *[[神武天皇即位紀元]]

{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 道臣命 | 画像 = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = [[歌川国芳]]『日本百将伝』より道臣命の想像図 | 時代 = [[弥生時代]]、[[上古]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = 道臣命 | 別名 = 日臣命 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[神武天皇]] | 氏族 = [[大伴氏|大伴連]]祖 | 父母 = 父：[[刺田比古命]]{{efn|「古屋家家譜」<ref>『甲斐国一之宮浅間神社誌』浅間神社 1979年,pp278-300。）資料篇所収。[[宝賀寿男]]『古代氏族系譜集成』（古代氏族研究会、1986年）も『古屋家家譜』の内容を採用している。</ref>}} | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[味日命]] | 特記事項 = }} [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|道臣命 ／『前賢故実』より]] [[ファイル:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|サムネイル|『大日本名将鑑道臣命』[[月岡芳年]]作]] '''道臣命'''（みちのおみのみこと）は、[[記紀]]に登場する古代[[日本]]の人物。初名は'''日臣命'''（ひのおみのみこと）。[[アメノオシヒ|天忍日命]]（あまのおしひのみこと）の後裔で[[大伴氏]]の祖。[[神武天皇]]の東征の先鋒を務め、神武天皇即位の際には宮門の警衛を務めた。
== 業績 == [[神武東征]]において、[[八咫烏]]の先導により[[久米部]]を率いて[[菟田]]（[[宇陀]]）への道を開いた。その功績により神武天皇から名を改めて道臣と名乗るよう言われる。
[[兄猾]]（[[兄宇迦斯]]、えうかし）が神武天皇に押機（おし）という罠を仕掛けた際、道臣は兄猾に「おまえが作った屋敷には、貴様自身が入れ」と述べ、剣の柄を握り、弓に矢をつがえ追い込み、兄猾は自身の罠に押しつぶされて死んだ。道臣はその死体を切り刻み、その地は宇陀の血原と呼ばれる。
国見岳で[[八十梟帥]]が討たれた後、天皇の密命によりその残党を討ち取った。まず忍坂の邑に大室を造り、精鋭を率いて残党と酒宴を開き、宴も酣になったとき道臣の[[久米歌]]を合図に兵たちは剣を抜き、残党を殲滅した。
その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。
神武天皇即位後はじめて政務を行う日、道臣命は諷歌（そえうた）・倒語（さかさご）をもって妖気を払った。神武天皇即位の翌年、東征の論幸行賞として築坂邑（橿原市鳥屋町付近）に宅地を賜わり、特に目をかけられたと記されている。
== 道臣命を祭神とする神社 == *[[刺田比古神社]]（[[和歌山市]]） *[[伴林氏神社]]（[[藤井寺市]]） *[[住吉大伴神社]] ([[京都市]]) *蹴裂権現社([[大分県]])
== 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇]] *[[大伴氏]] *[[神武景気]] *[[神武天皇即位紀元]]
==脚注== {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}}
==外部リンク== *[https://okanomiya.com/ 刺田比古神社]

その前に、神武天皇自ら[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊尊]]を顕斎するときにその斎主に任じられ「厳媛（いづひめ）」の号を授けられた（道臣命は男性であるが、女性の名をつけたのは、神を祀るのは女性の役目であったことの名残とみられる）。

== 道臣命を祭神とする神社 == *[[刺田比古神社]]（[[和歌山市]]） *[[伴林氏神社]]（[[藤井寺市]]） *[[住吉大伴神社]] ([[京都市]]) *蹴裂権現社([[大分県]])

revision.text:
{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During the {{ill|Jinmu Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sashitahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Jinmu Economy|ja|神武景気}} * {{ill|Jinmu Ascension Era|ja|神武天皇即位紀元}}
== Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://okanomiya.com/ Official website of Sashitahiko Shrine]

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During the {{ill|Jinmu Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}, Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Jinmu Economy|ja|神武景気}} * {{ill|Japanese imperial year|ja|神武天皇即位紀元}}
== Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://okanomiya.com/ Official website of Sashitahiko Shrine]

[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Jinmu Economy|ja|神武景気}} * {{ill|Japanese imperial year|ja|神武天皇即位紀元}}

He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Jinmu Economy|ja|神武景気}} * {{ill|Japanese imperial year|ja|神武天皇即位紀元}}

'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Jinmu Economy|ja|神武景気}} * {{ill|Japanese imperial year|ja|神武天皇即位紀元}}

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Jinmu Economy|ja|神武景気}} * {{ill|Japanese imperial year|ja|神武天皇即位紀元}}
== Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://okanomiya.com/ Official website of Sashitahiko Shrine]

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).

'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).

Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).

Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

{{Infobox noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})

{{Infobox noble | name = Michinoomi no Mikoto | image = 道臣命 Michinoomi no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | image_size = | caption = Imaginary portrait of Michinoomi no Mikoto by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}}, from ''Nihon Hyakushōden'' | era = [[Ancient Japan|Ancient]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Hinoomi no Mikoto | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | resting_place = | rank = | allegiance = [[Emperor Jimmu]] | clan = {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} (Ōtomo no Muraji) | parents = Father: {{ill|Sashitahiko no Mikoto|ja|刺田比古命}}{{efn|According to the ''Koya-ke Genealogy''<ref>''Kōkai-koku Ichinomiya Asama Jinja-shi,'' Asama Shrine, 1979, pp. 278-300. Toshiho Hoga's ''Collection of Ancient Clan Genealogies'' (1986) also references this content.</ref>}} | siblings = | spouse = | children = {{ill|Ajihime no Mikoto|ja|味日命}} | notable_achievements = }}
[[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto.jpg|thumb|220px|Michinoomi no Mikoto / from ''Zenkō Kojitsu'']] [[File:Michinoomi no Mikoto following a crow to the enemy's lair LACMA M.2000.105.97.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Michinoomi no Mikoto in ''Dai Nippon Meishōkan'' by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|ja|月岡芳年}}]]
'''Michinoomi no Mikoto''' (道臣命) is a legendary figure from ancient [[Japan]], mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. Initially named '''Hinoomi no Mikoto''' (日臣命), he was a descendant of {{ill|Amenooshihi no Mikoto|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and the ancestor of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}. He served as a vanguard in {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}’s eastern campaign and later as a palace guard upon Emperor Jimmu’s accession.
== Achievements ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takami-musubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}} * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} * {{ill|Jinmu Economy|ja|神武景気}} * {{ill|Japanese imperial year|ja|神武天皇即位紀元}}
== Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}}

During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).

During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]], Michinoomi led the {{ill|Kumebe|ja|久米部}} warriors, guided by the divine {{ill|Yatagarasu|ja|八咫烏}}, to open a route from {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}} to Uda (modern {{ill|Uda|ja|宇陀}}). For his accomplishments, Emperor Jimmu bestowed upon him the name "Michinoomi."
When {{ill|Eukashi|ja|兄猾}} (Eukashi no Kami) attempted to ensnare Emperor Jimmu in a trap called "Oshi," Michinoomi confronted him, declaring, "Enter the house you built for yourself." With sword and bow ready, he forced Eukashi into his trap, where Eukashi perished. Michinoomi then dismembered Eukashi’s body, and the site became known as ''Chi-no-Hara'' (Blood Field) in Uda.
After the defeat of the {{ill|Eighty Bandit Chiefs|ja|八十梟帥}} at Kunimi-dake, Michinoomi was tasked with eradicating their remnants. He constructed a grand chamber in the village of Osaka and lured the bandits to a banquet. At the height of the feast, he signaled his troops with a {{ill|Kumebushi|ja|久米節}} war song, and they drew their swords to annihilate the enemy.
Earlier, Michinoomi had been appointed by Emperor Jimmu to serve as a priest for the worship of {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊尊}}, earning the honorary title ''Izuhime'' (厳媛). Despite being male, this title reflects an ancient tradition of women officiating rituals.
Upon Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement, Michinoomi performed ceremonial songs and rituals to purify negative energies. In recognition of his service, he was granted a residence in ''Tsukisaka'' (present-day Kashihara, Nara Prefecture).
== Shrines Dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto == * {{ill|Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine}} ({{ill|Wakayama|ja|和歌山市}}) * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} ({{ill|Fujidera|ja|藤井寺市}}) * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine|ja|住吉大伴神社}} ({{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}) * Kerasaki Gongen Shrine ({{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}})
